title: Chiacchiere
subtitle: Italian carnival fried pastry ribbons dusted with icing sugar
excerpt: Chiacchiere are thin sheets of egg and wine dough, cut into ribbons and fried until they puff and blister. Served in a pile dusted with icing sugar, they are the Italian equivalent of angel wings or fried dough ribbons made across many European carnival traditions. Crisp while warm, soft the next day.
sourceNotes: Chiacchiere (Lombardy), also called frappe (Lazio), cenci (Tuscany), and galani (Venice), are fried pastry ribbons eaten across Italy during Carnival, the pre-Lent festival period. Regional names and small recipe variations exist, but the dough is always thin, enriched with egg and a small amount of wine or grappa, and the result is always deep-fried and dusted with icing sugar. The tradition has roots in ancient Roman libum cakes. The recipe follows the standard northern Italian domestic tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and eat on the same day. Chiacchiere lose their crispness quickly. Best eaten within 2 hours of frying.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: deep-fried at 170 to 175 degrees C; use an instant-read thermometer to check the oil

glossaryTerms.definition: Chiacchiere dough must rest for at least 30 minutes before rolling. The gluten relaxes during the rest, which allows the dough to be rolled extremely thin without springing back. Without resting, the sheets will be uneven and the pastry will be tough rather than crisp and blistered after frying.

content.text: Chiacchiere are one of the simplest fried pastries. The dough is quick to make, and rolling it as thin as possible is the only technique that matters. The
is what makes it possible to roll the sheets thin enough to blister in the oil.

Combine 300 g plain flour, 30 g caster sugar, and 3 g salt in a bowl. Add 2 eggs, 40 g melted butter, and 60 ml white wine. Mix to a firm dough. Turn out and knead for 3 minutes until smooth. Wrap and rest at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Heat 1 litre vegetable oil in a deep fryer or deep saucepan to 170 to 175 degrees C. Divide the dough into 4 pieces. Roll each piece on a floured surface as thin as you can manage, ideally 1 to 2 mm. Cut into irregular strips or rectangles, roughly 4 cm wide and 12 cm long. Optionally cut a slit down the middle of each strip and pull one end through the slit to twist it. Fry a few at a time for 1 to 2 minutes, turning once, until pale golden and blistered. Remove with a spider strainer and drain on a baking tray lined with baking paper. Continue with remaining dough. Dust generously with icing sugar and serve immediately.

items.problem: Pastry does not blister in the oil
items.solution: The dough was not rolled thin enough. Aim for 1 to 2 mm. A pasta machine helps enormously here; use it at the second-thinnest setting. Thick dough fries without the trapped steam that causes blistering.
Too many pieces went in at once. Fry no more than 3 to 4 at a time so the oil maintains its temperature. Let it come back up between batches.
